sets.passage: When a new student named Tom joined our class, the first thing everyone noticed was his (1)___. He used a wheelchair, and at first some classmates did not know how to (2)___ around him. A few, ashamed to admit it, even quietly (3)___ him, unsure of what to say. I felt the same awkwardness, until one day I simply sat down beside him and started (4)___. To my surprise, Tom was funny, clever and full of (5)___. Within minutes I had forgotten all about the wheelchair and was simply talking to a new (6)___. As we grew closer, I began to notice the small daily (7)___ he faced that the rest of us never thought about: doorways too narrow, steps with no ramp, tables too high. So a few of us decided to (8)___. We spoke to the school, and over the next term several ramps were (9)___ and a heavy door was made automatic. The changes helped Tom, but they quietly helped (10)___ too, from the cleaner with her trolley to a teacher on crutches. Through Tom, I learned a lesson I have never (11)___: that a person is never defined by what their body cannot (12)___. The barriers that had made his life hard were not in him but in the world around him, and those barriers could be (13)___. Tom is now one of my closest friends. He taught me that true (14)___ is not pity, but simply treating someone as the equal they have always (15)___.

sets.passage: One afternoon, while playing football in the street, I kicked the ball far too hard and sent it crashing through our neighbour Mr. Grey's (1)___. The sound of breaking glass made my heart (2)___. My first instinct was to (3)___: no one had seen me, and I could simply walk away and pretend it had been someone (4)___. For a long, guilty minute I stood frozen, the ball lying among the broken glass on his lawn. Then I thought of how my parents had always taught me to be (5)___, especially when it was hard. So, with a dry mouth and shaking hands, I walked to Mr. Grey's door and (6)___ the bell. When he answered, I forced myself to look him in the eye and (7)___ what I had done. I expected him to be (8)___. Instead, his stern face slowly softened into a small smile. He told me that the window could easily be (9)___, but that honesty like mine was much (10)___ to find. He even refused to let my parents pay for all of it, saying my courage in owning up was worth a (11)___. I offered to do jobs in his garden to make up for the (12)___, and over that summer, the grumpy old neighbour I had always feared became an unlikely (13)___. Looking back, I am so glad I did not run away. That broken window taught me that admitting a mistake is far harder than (14)___ it, but that honesty, in the end, costs us far less than the (15)___ we would otherwise carry.

sets.passage: As a child, I was convinced I had no (1)___ for art. My drawings were clumsy, and whenever I tried to paint what I saw, the result looked nothing like (2)___. So I gave up, telling myself that art was a gift you were either born with or (3)___. Years later, bored one rainy weekend, I picked up a pencil and, with no expectations, began to (4)___ the cup on my desk. It was, predictably, a (5)___. Slowly, very slowly, my hand learned to follow my eye. After a year of daily practice, I flipped back to my first clumsy sketches and could hardly believe the (10)___. sets.passage: As part of a school programme, I was sent, rather (1)___, to spend an afternoon each week at a local care home. I expected it to be (2)___ and a little depressing. Instead, it became the highlight of my (3)___. There I was paired with Mr. Alvarez, a man of ninety with a sharp mind and a (4)___ sense of humour. We were separated by more than seventy years, yet we became unlikely (5)___. Every week, over cups of weak tea, he told me stories of a world I had only read about in (6)___: of long sea voyages, of cities that no longer existed, of a life crowded with adventure and (7)___. In return, I taught him to send messages to his great-grandchildren on a tablet, and we laughed endlessly at his typing (8)___. What surprised me most was how much I (9)___ from these afternoons. In a culture that worships youth, I had unthinkingly assumed that the old had little to (10)___ me. Mr. Alvarez gently proved me (11)___. His stories gave me a longer view of life, and his calm in the face of age and loss taught me more about (12)___ than any lesson at school. When he passed away, I grieved as if I had lost a member of my own (13)___. But I was left with a (14)___: that wisdom and friendship know no age, and that we lose a great deal when we shut the old away and stop (15)___ to them.

sets.passage: My uncle is what most people would call a (1)___. His workshop is piled high with broken chairs, rusty tools and bits of metal that anyone else would throw (2)___. As a boy, I thought it was all just (3)___. Then one summer, he showed me what he actually (4)___ with it. From a heap of scrap, his hands could shape a beautiful lamp, a clever toy, or a small metal bird so lifelike it seemed about to (5)___ away. 'Nothing is really (6)___,' he told me, 'only waiting to become something else.' He offered to teach me, and I spent that summer at his side, learning to (7)___ value where others saw only junk. It was harder than it looked: my first attempts were (8)___, and more than once I wanted to give up. But slowly I began to see the world through his (9)___. A cracked teapot became a flowerpot; an old ladder became a (10)___. I learned that creativity is not about having expensive (11)___, but about seeing (12)___ in what others ignore. That lesson reached far beyond his workshop. I began to look at problems, and even at difficult people, differently, asking not 'what is wrong with this?' but 'what could this (13)___?'
